facebook.texto: Why are women suddenly mixing this $3 grocery-store gelatin every night? 👀 It started as a weird kitchen trend… now thousands swear by the same simple recipe. There's one specific way you have to prepare it — most people do it wrong. Watch the short video to see exactly how it works 👇

analise_criativa.avatar: Frame 1: blonde woman in denim shirt preparing a dark red drink in a modern kitchen. Frame 2: brunette woman holding a baby with a red gelatin drink visible on the counter in front of her — strong relatable mom figure. Frame 3: hands stirring something in a pan on a stove.
analise_criativa.editing: Vertical mobile-first video, raw and unpolished UGC-style filming. Natural home lighting, no heavy editing or graphics. Multiple women shown suggesting social proof through variety of characters.
analise_criativa.observation: The ad uses two distinct women across frames, which may suggest a compilation or split-test format within one video. The baby-holding frame (15s) is a strong emotional scroll-stopper — postpartum weight loss angle implied without being stated. No product shown explicitly.
analise_criativa.highlight: The mom-with-baby frame at 15s is highly disruptive and emotionally resonant — postpartum audience will self-identify instantly. The $3 grocery-store framing lowers barrier and builds mass-market curiosity. 'Most people do it wrong' is a classic curiosity trigger that drives click-through.

analise_criativa.avatar: Frame 1: blonde woman in denim shirt preparing a dark red drink in a modern kitchen. Frame 2: brunette woman holding a baby with a red gelatin drink on the counter — strong relatable mom figure. Frame 3: hands stirring in a pan on a stove.
analise_criativa.editing: Identical creative to AD 3 — vertical mobile-first, raw UGC-style, natural home lighting. No heavy post-production.
analise_criativa.observation: This is the same video creative as AD 3, run under a different page name (Mary Taylor vs unknown page). Minor copy variation: adds 'Watch Now', 'Watch before this video gets removed', and 'See details' CTAs. The 'before this video gets removed' urgency line is an aggressive DR tactic worth noting.
analise_criativa.highlight: The addition of 'Watch before this video gets removed' is a strong urgency/scarcity trigger that could meaningfully improve CTR over the AD 3 version. Worth A/B testing this copy variant directly against AD 3 to measure lift.

analise_criativa.avatar: Frame 1: blonde woman in denim shirt preparing a dark red drink in a modern kitchen. Frame 2: brunette woman holding a baby with a red gelatin drink on the counter. Frame 3: hands stirring in a pan on a stove.
analise_criativa.editing: Identical visual creative to AD 3 and AD 4 — vertical, raw UGC-style, natural home lighting.
analise_criativa.observation: Third iteration of the same base video creative under the Mary Taylor page. Copy differs from AD 4 only by omitting 'See details' at the end. Minimal differentiation from AD 4 — likely a split-test on the CTA tail copy.
analise_criativa.highlight: Running the same creative across multiple pages and slight copy variants is a signal the advertiser is actively scaling this angle. The gelatin recipe hook combined with the mom-with-baby visual is a proven pattern worth replicating or iterating on for UK and Germany markets with localized copy.

analise_criativa.avatar: Two women shown across frames: first a blonde woman in a denim shirt preparing a dark drink in a bright kitchen, second a brunette woman holding a baby with a bowl of red gelatin in front of her — both in domestic kitchen settings
analise_criativa.editing: Raw, handheld UGC-style footage with natural lighting; cuts between multiple real women performing the same recipe ritual, reinforcing social proof through variety of avatars
analise_criativa.observation: The use of two distinct women in the same ad is unusual — it functions almost like a collage of testimonials compressed into one video, amplifying the 'thousands of women are doing this' hook from the copy. The final frame showing only hands stirring in a pan removes the human face, which slightly weakens scroll-stop but reinforces the recipe/how-to angle
analise_criativa.highlight: Strong convergence between copy and visuals — the $3 gelatin hook is immediately visualised. The mother-with-baby frame is emotionally powerful and highly relatable for the postpartum/busy-mom segment. 'Watch before this video gets removed' urgency CTA is a proven DR trigger. Worth split-testing the baby frame as a standalone static thumbnail.

There's one specific way you have to prepare it — most people do it wrong. Watch the short video to see exactly how it works 👇

analise_criativa.avatar: Two women shown across frames: blonde woman in denim shirt preparing a dark drink in a kitchen, and a brunette woman holding a baby with a red gelatin bowl in front of her
analise_criativa.editing: Same raw handheld UGC-style footage as Ad 1, natural lighting, multi-avatar structure compressed into one video
analise_criativa.observation: Identical creative to Ad 1 but running from an unknown page — likely a dark post or whitelist test. Copy is slightly shorter, omitting the 'Watch before this video gets removed' urgency line, which may reduce CTR compared to Ad 1
analise_criativa.highlight: Running the same creative from an anonymous page is a common DR tactic to test audience response without brand association. The absence of the removal-urgency CTA makes this a useful A/B variant to measure impact of that line on click-through.

analise_criativa.avatar: Two women across frames: blonde woman in denim shirt in a bright kitchen preparing a dark drink, brunette woman holding a baby with a red gelatin bowl — same as Ads 1 and 2
analise_criativa.editing: Identical raw handheld UGC footage, natural light, multi-avatar structure
analise_criativa.observation: Marked as 'Inactive' in the copy text — this is the same creative as Ads 1 and 2, likely a paused or killed variant from the same test batch. The word 'Inactive' appearing in the ad text body is a data capture artefact and should be noted as a copy error if ever reactivated
analise_criativa.highlight: Being the inactive version of the top-running creative makes this useful as a historical benchmark. If reactivated, remove the 'Inactive' text artefact before launch.

facebook.texto: I was always the hungry one. Everyone in my family knew it. "Linda could out-eat her brothers," my mom used to say. She thought it was funny. I didn't. Because while everyone laughed, I was the eight-year-old sneaking back to the kitchen after dinner. The teenager who finished her plate first and watched everyone else eat slowly, like they had all the time in the world. The woman in her twenties who could never understand how her friends "forgot to eat lunch." Forgot to eat? I have never forgott

analise_criativa.avatar: Linda Carver — appears as a middle-aged woman in a warm domestic setting (visible in profile picture), positioned as a relatable everyday woman with a lifelong struggle with hunger and appetite rather than willpower
analise_criativa.editing: Static image ad formatted as a long-form personal confession post; Facebook native feed style with profile name and sponsored label visible; the ad image partially shows a woman near a food spread in dim warm lighting, reinforcing the emotional overeating narrative
analise_criativa.observation: The keyword 'natural ozempic' signals the audience is already GLP-1 aware and looking for alternatives — the copy smartly never mentions Ozempic by name but the landing page URL description references GLP-1 fullness hormone, creating a bridge. The 'I was always the hungry one' opening is a strong identity hook that targets emotional eaters specifically rather than general weight loss seekers
analise_criativa.highlight: Exceptionally strong long-form copy with deep emotional resonance — the childhood memory angle ('eight-year-old sneaking back to the kitchen') is rare and highly specific, which builds credibility and stops scroll for the right avatar. The hunger/appetite angle differentiates from generic fat-burning messaging. High potential for the emotional-overeater segment. Worth testing as a video with voiceover reading this copy.

analise_criativa.avatar: Two different women shown in kitchen settings — first a blonde woman preparing a drink at a counter, second a dark-haired woman holding a baby with a red gelatin drink in front of her. Third frame shows hands stirring something in a pan on a stove.
analise_criativa.editing: Raw, casual UGC-style footage. Vertical format, natural lighting, no heavy editing or text overlays visible in frames. Feels authentic and unpolished, consistent with organic-style content.
analise_criativa.observation: Ad uses two distinct women across frames which could indicate a compilation of multiple UGC clips edited together. The baby in the second frame adds a strong relatability angle for postpartum weight loss audience. Copy references '$3 grocery-store gelatin' which is a US price point but ad targets GB — minor localisation mismatch.
analise_criativa.highlight: The postpartum visual (woman holding baby next to gelatin drink) is a powerful scroll-stopper for the target demographic. The recipe-based hook with scarcity framing ('watch before this video gets removed') is a proven DR pattern. Gelatin keyword alignment with visuals is tight. Worth testing a dedicated postpartum angle variant.

analise_criativa.avatar: Two different women shown in kitchen settings — first a blonde woman preparing a drink at a counter, second a dark-haired woman holding a baby with a red gelatin drink in front of her. Third frame shows hands stirring something in a pan on a stove.
analise_criativa.editing: Raw, casual UGC-style footage. Vertical format, natural lighting, no heavy editing or text overlays visible in frames. Identical visual content to Ad 1.
analise_criativa.observation: This ad is functionally identical to Ad 1 in both visuals and copy. The only difference in the text is the addition of 'See details System status' at the end, likely a platform artefact. Running two near-identical creatives simultaneously under the same page may cause audience overlap and internal competition. Days active differ by 1 day suggesting a duplicate test or accidental relaunch.
analise_criativa.highlight: Same strong postpartum visual and recipe hook as Ad 1. If performance data is available, comparing the two identical creatives could reveal delivery or auction insights. No new creative learning expected from this duplicate.

repeated_creatives.description: Identical video/image sequence across all frames: blonde woman in denim shirt preparing a dark red drink in a modern kitchen, brunette woman holding a baby with a red gelatin bowl on the counter, and hands stirring in a pan on a stove. Same copy, same keyword, same format — clear single original creative duplicated across pages and accounts.

frequent_avatars.type: Relatable domestic mom — brunette or blonde woman in a home kitchen setting, often shown with a baby or child nearby, signaling a busy mother who discovers a simple affordable solution rather than a diet or program

Lifelong hunger sufferer — middle-aged everyday woman who frames her weight struggle as a biological appetite problem, not a willpower failure, positioning herself as finally having found a natural solution

facebook_cross_analysis.dominant_mechanism: Grocery-store gelatin as a nightly metabolic or appetite-suppressing ritual — framed as a simple, cheap kitchen recipe that triggers weight loss through a specific preparation method, implying a gut or collagen-based mechanism
facebook_cross_analysis.emerging_angle: Natural Ozempic framing via UGC personal narrative — positioning a supplement as a GLP-1 appetite-regulation alternative for women who have biologically struggled with hunger their whole lives, tapping into the Ozempic cultural moment without pharma risk
facebook_cross_analysis.market_signal: US and UK markets are heavily stress-testing a single gelatin recipe creative across multiple pages and accounts, indicating strong early performance data on the curiosity-gap kitchen trend hook targeting moms, while simultaneously probing the natural Ozempic narrative as a high-intent alternative angle
